This section preserves the fictional corporate knowledge base I built as a documentation design exercise. The product names are placeholders. The value is in the structure: onboarding product documentation support content tutorials how-to guides explanations reference pages API documentation localization readiness

A technical writing portfolio needs evidence. If I do not have permission to publish real company documents, I can still create realistic documentation samples that show my structure, writing judgment, and tooling ability.

Hiring teams can look at this section to evaluate: whether the navigation is understandable whether content types are separated clearly whether product docs have consistent patterns whether the site feels like a maintainable knowledge base whether the API and support areas are discoverable

Replace placeholder pages with polished samples. Add a content model for product docs. Add writing guidelines for the fictional docs team. Add accessibility and localization notes. Add a documentation audit page explaining decisions.

Q: What is the default password? A: admin / admin Q: How to factory reset? A: Press and hold reset button for 10+ seconds. Q: Device not getting IP address? A: Check cable, PoE switch, or set static IP. Q: SIP registration failed? A: Check SIP server address, credentials, and firewall. Q: How to update firmware? A: Download latest firmware from Fanvil official site and upload via web interface. Still not solved? Contact Fanvil Support

API documentation helps developers understand how to integrate with a product or service. Good API documentation is not only endpoint reference. It also includes authentication, concepts, quickstarts, examples, error handling, limits, SDKs, and troubleshooting.
Overview Authentication Quickstart Endpoint reference Request examples Response examples Error codes Rate limits SDK guides Changelog
API documentation has two audiences: humans who need conceptual understanding machines and tools that need precise schemas That means the documentation needs both clarity and strict accuracy.

Learn REST API documentation patterns. Practice request and response examples. Understand OpenAPI and schema-first documentation. Learn how API docs fit into CI/CD. Build sample API reference pages in this portfolio.

AI changes documentation work, but it does not remove the need for documentation strategy. AI can help draft, summarize, translate, classify, and search content. But the human writer still needs to define the audience, structure, quality bar, source of truth, and maintenance process.
draft first versions summarize technical material generate examples compare documentation structures find unclear wording create content templates support multilingual workflows
product accuracy information architecture user research legal and safety risk content governance voice and tone prioritization
The future technical writer will not only write documents. They will design knowledge systems where humans and AI can both find, maintain, and reuse accurate information.

Docs-as-code means treating documentation like software. Instead of writing only in a traditional document editor, writers use tools such as Git, Markdown, static site generators, code review, automated checks, and deployment pipelines.

Docs-as-code is important for modern technical writers because many product teams already work this way. It helps documentation teams: collaborate with engineers review content changes in pull requests track version history publish automatically keep docs closer to the product automate quality checks
Markdown for writing Docusaurus for publishing Git and GitHub for version control GitHub Pages for deployment Localhost for previewing changes npm scripts for build and deploy
CI/CD basics OpenAPI generation linting documentation automated link checking content review workflows branch and pull request strategy
Official technical documentation and support resources for Fanvil Link Technology products.
Quick Start Guide X Series IP Phones Door Phones General FAQ Official Resources Downloads | Fanvil Official Site
DITA is a structured authoring standard often used for technical documentation. The important idea is that content can be modular, typed, reusable, and publishable across different outputs.
DITA helps documentation teams manage: topic-based writing content reuse conditional publishing consistent structure large documentation sets multi-output publishing

I am learning DITA together with Oxygen XML Editor because many enterprise documentation teams still need structured authoring skills. My goal is not only to know the syntax. I want to understand when DITA is useful, when Markdown is enough, and how structured content changes the writer's thinking.

DITA and docs-as-code are not enemies. They are different answers to different documentation problems. Approach Good for DITA large enterprise docs, reuse, conditional publishing, strict structure Markdown/docs-as-code developer docs, fast publishing, Git workflows, lightweight sites The stronger technical writer should understand both worlds.

Diataxis is a documentation framework that separates content by user need. It is useful because users do not always need the same kind of help. Sometimes they want to learn. Sometimes they want to complete a task. Sometimes they want to understand a concept. Sometimes they only need exact reference information.

Type User need Example Tutorial I want to learn by doing. Pair your first device. How-to guide I want to complete a task. Update firmware. Explanation I want to understand why. How wireless pairing works. Reference I need exact facts. API parameters or product specifications.

Without a framework, documentation can become a messy pile of pages. With a framework, each page has a job. For a portfolio, Diataxis is also a signal. It shows that the writer can think about documentation as a system, not only as isolated articles.
I want to use Diataxis to: design knowledge bases audit confusing documentation decide what kind of page a user needs improve navigation labels separate conceptual learning from task instructions
documents.s: How I want to use it

documents.t: Technical writing is the practice of helping people use, understand, and trust technical systems. It is not only grammar or translation. It includes user research, information architecture, product understanding, content design, writing, editing, publishing, and maintenance.

Technical writing turns complex product knowledge into usable guidance for a specific audience. The writer needs to understand: who the user is what task the user wants to complete what the user already knows what the system can and cannot do what risks or mistakes must be prevented how the content will be maintained

Technical writing can grow into several related roles: Field Focus Information architecture Organizing content so people can find and understand it. Documentation engineering Building and maintaining documentation systems and publishing workflows. Content strategy Planning content according to business goals and user needs. UX writing Writing interface text that guides product behavior. API documentation Helping developers understand and integrate with software systems. Knowledge management Capturing, organizing, and maintaining institutional knowledge.
How can a technical writer become more valuable in the AI era? My current answer: by moving from page-level writing to system-level documentation strategy.
This roadmap records my learning path toward technical writing, documentation engineering, information architecture, content strategy, UX writing, and AI documentation consulting.
I am building a public portfolio while learning the tools and thinking patterns used by modern documentation teams.

Track Why it matters Current focus Technical writing Core professional foundation clarity, structure, task writing Information architecture Makes large docs usable navigation, content models, Diataxis Docs-as-code Connects writing with engineering teams Git, Docusaurus, GitHub Pages API documentation Opens developer documentation roles REST, examples, schemas Structured authoring Supports enterprise documentation DITA, Oxygen XML Editor UX writing Connects documentation with product experience error messages, onboarding copy AI documentation Creates future opportunity AI workflows, knowledge systems
Turn this site into a credible job-search portfolio. Add 3-5 polished case studies. Build one API documentation sample. Add real learning notes every week. Prepare role-specific resume language.
I want to grow from writer to documentation strategist: someone who can design knowledge systems, improve product communication, and help companies build better documentation workflows with AI.
This page maps my current and future skills.

Skill Use DITA Structured authoring and enterprise documentation. Markdown Lightweight documentation writing. Oxygen XML Editor DITA authoring and XML editing. HTML Web content structure. CSS Visual presentation and layout refinement. Docusaurus Static documentation websites and knowledge bases. GitHub Pages Public deployment for portfolio projects.

Skill Why I am learning it Git Work with docs-as-code teams and track documentation changes. CI/CD Understand automated publishing and quality checks. API documentation Qualify for developer documentation roles. OpenAPI Connect API schemas with generated documentation. Accessibility Make documentation usable for more people. AI workflows Improve documentation quality and productivity.
information architecture content strategy UX writing documentation audit content modeling style guide thinking stakeholder communication technical learning
This sample shows the kind of structure I want to use for developer-facing API documentation.
GET /v1/devices/{deviceId} Retrieves metadata and connection status for a device.
Name Type Required Description deviceId string Yes Unique identifier of the device.

Field Type Description id string Unique device identifier. name string Human-readable device name. status string Current connection status. Possible values: online, offline, unknown. firmwareVersion string Installed firmware version. lastSeenAt string ISO 8601 timestamp for the latest device heartbeat.

Good API documentation should: make authentication and error handling easy to find include request and response examples explain required fields before optional fields separate concepts from endpoint reference show realistic values instead of abstract placeholders
I am building this site because I want to move toward a stronger technical communication career.

I am using this transition period to learn, build, and reposition myself for better opportunities. The goal is not only to find the next job. The goal is to build proof that I can think and work like a documentation professional.

My target identity: A technical communication professional who can design modern documentation systems, write clearly, learn technical domains quickly, and help teams organize knowledge in the AI era.
technical writer information architect documentation engineer content strategist UX writer API documentation writer documentation consultant

In the long term, I want this portfolio to help me reach: better companies stronger documentation teams higher salary opportunities consulting opportunities AI documentation strategy work thought leadership in technical communication

This site should become: a portfolio for job interviews a learning log for technical writing and AI a public knowledge base a writing sample a proof of docs-as-code ability a place to practice documentation strategy

This site includes a fictional corporate documentation portal. The product names are not the main point. The purpose is to demonstrate how I can design a mature documentation system that feels similar to a company knowledge base.

Build a professional documentation website that shows: clear navigation for different user needs product documentation structure onboarding and quickstart content tutorial, how-to, explanation, and reference pages support and troubleshooting flows API documentation sections localization readiness GitHub Pages deployment

The documentation lab is organized around user intent: Area User question Content type Getting Started How do I begin? onboarding, prerequisites, quickstart Products What does this product support? overview, model pages, datasheets, manuals Learn How do I perform or understand tasks? tutorials, how-to guides, explanation, reference Support How do I solve problems? FAQ, troubleshooting, contact API How do developers integrate? authentication, endpoints, errors

The learning section can be mapped to Diataxis: Tutorials help users learn through guided practice. How-to guides help users complete a specific task. Explanation helps users understand concepts and decisions. Reference gives stable factual information. This separation is important because users visit documentation with different levels of experience and different goals.

This case study demonstrates my ability to: create a scalable documentation structure separate learning content from support content write navigation labels that match user intent build a docs-as-code site with Docusaurus think beyond individual pages and design a documentation system

Add a content model for product manual pages. Add reusable template pages for datasheets and troubleshooting. Add a documentation governance page. Add metrics for search terms, top support issues, and content gaps. Add a bilingual strategy for English and Chinese pages.

This portfolio is a public record of my transition into technical writing, documentation engineering, information architecture, content strategy, and UX writing. The site is built with Docusaurus because the tool itself is part of the story. A documentation portfolio should not only display writing samples. It should show how the writer thinks about structure, navigation, publishing, maintenance, and user journeys.

Capability Evidence on this site Documentation architecture A corporate-style knowledge base with onboarding, product docs, support docs, tutorials, reference, and API content. Structured writing Pages organized around user goals, content types, and reusable patterns. Docs-as-code learning GitHub Pages, Docusaurus, Markdown, Git, build/deploy workflow, and local development. Technical communication thinking Notes about Diataxis, DITA, API documentation, UX writing, and AI-assisted documentation. Career direction A learning roadmap that connects current skills with future roles.

UX writing is where technical accuracy meets product behavior. The goal is not to sound clever. The goal is to help users understand what is happening and what to do next.
The firmware update could not finish because the device lost connection. Reconnect the device, then try again.
It names the problem. It explains the likely cause. It gives the next action. It avoids blaming the user.
Remove this device from your workspace? Historical logs will stay available, but the device will stop syncing new data.
Clear UX writing answers three questions: What is happening? Why does it matter? What can I do next?
Model: AH-1 · Firmware: v1.0.2 · Document version: 1.2
Item Quantity AH-1 Headphones × 1 USB-C Charging Cable (1 m) × 1 3.5 mm Audio Cable × 1 Carry Pouch × 1 Quick Install Guide × 1

Control Action Power button (hold 2 s) Power on / off Power button (press) Play / Pause Volume + / − Adjust volume ANC button Toggle noise cancellation Multifunction button (hold 2 s) Enter pairing mode

Connect the USB-C cable to the charging port on the left earcup. Connect the other end to a USB power source (5 V / 1 A minimum). The LED indicator turns red while charging and white when fully charged. Full charge time: approximately 2 hours. note Do not use the headphones during the first full charge cycle.

Power on the headphones by holding the Power button for 2 seconds. Hold the Multifunction button for 2 seconds until the LED flashes blue. On your device, open Bluetooth Settings and select Aeyron AH-1. Pairing is confirmed when the LED turns solid white and you hear a tone. tip The AH-1 can store up to 3 paired devices. To switch devices, hold the Multifunction button for 2 seconds on a previously paired device.
LED State Meaning Solid white Powered on, connected Flashing blue Pairing mode Flashing white Connecting to last device Solid red Charging Flashing red Battery low (< 10%) Off Powered off

Parameter Value Driver size 40 mm dynamic Frequency response 20 Hz – 20 kHz Impedance 32 Ω Bluetooth version 5.3 Wireless range Up to 10 m Battery capacity 500 mAh Playback time Up to 30 hours (ANC off) Charge time ~2 hours Weight 248 g

warning Keep the headphones away from water and high humidity. This product is not waterproof. danger Listening at high volumes for extended periods may cause permanent hearing damage. Keep volume at a safe level.
Quick Install Guide — one-page setup reference Datasheet — full technical specifications Troubleshooting — Headphones Pairing Tutorial

This article explains how to wire different types of electric locks to Fanvil i60 and i60K door phones. It helps prevent wiring mistakes that may lead to unlock failures or safety risks. It also provides a brief introduction to fail-safe and fail-secure locks, along with clear wiring diagrams and typical usage scenarios.

There are several common types of electric locks used in access control systems: Figure 1-1 dddd Lock Type Fail Mode Typical Scenarios Electromagnetic Lock Fail-safe Offices, emergency exits Electric Bolt Lock Fail-safe / Fail-secure Glass doors, secure rooms Electric Rim Lock Fail-secure Apartments, residential doors

Before wiring, it is critical to understand how your lock behaves during a power loss. Choose Fail-safe for safety-critical areas to ensure that people can exit during power failure. Choose Fail-secure for high-security areas to keep the door locked during power loss.
Status: The door unlocks when power is lost; and remains locked when powered. Scenarios: Emergency exits, fire escape routes, office glass doors, etc.
Status: The door remains locked during a power outage; and unlocks when powered. Scenarios: Perimeter gates, server room, apartment entrances, etc.
Door locks can be powered by 12 VDC or a power supply unit (PSU). Fail-safe lock Fail-secure lock note ddd caution ddd warning ddd danger ddd tip ddd

You have just learned the basics of Docusaurus and made some changes to the initial template. Docusaurus has much more to offer! Have 5 more minutes? Take a look at versioning and i18n. Anything unclear or buggy in this tutorial? Please report it!

Read the official documentation Modify your site configuration with docusaurus.config.js Add navbar and footer items with themeConfig Add a custom Design and Layout Add a search bar Find inspirations in the Docusaurus showcase Get involved in the Docusaurus Community
Docusaurus creates a page for each blog post, but also a blog index page, a tag system, an RSS feed...

Create a file at blog/2021-02-28-greetings.md: blog/2021-02-28-greetings.md --- slug: greetings title: Greetings! authors: - name: Joel Marcey title: Co-creator of Docusaurus 1 url: https://github.com/JoelMarcey image_url: https://github.com/JoelMarcey.png - name: Sébastien Lorber title: Docusaurus maintainer url: https://sebastienlorber.com image_url: https://github.com/slorber.png tags: [greetings] --- Congratulations, you have made your first post! Feel free to play around and edit this post as much as you like. A new blog post is now available at http://localhost:3000/blog/greetings.
Documents are groups of pages connected through: a sidebar previous/next navigation versioning
Create a Markdown file at docs/hello.md: docs/hello.md # Hello This is my **first Docusaurus document**! A new document is now available at http://localhost:3000/docs/hello.

Docusaurus automatically creates a sidebar from the docs folder. Add metadata to customize the sidebar label and position: docs/hello.md --- sidebar_label: 'Hi!' sidebar_position: 3 --- # Hello This is my **first Docusaurus document**! It is also possible to create your sidebar explicitly in sidebars.js: sidebars.js export default { tutorialSidebar: [ 'intro', 'hello', { type: 'category', label: 'Tutorial', items: ['tutorial-basics/create-a-document'], }, ], };
Add Markdown or React files to src/pages to create a standalone page: src/pages/index.js → localhost:3000/ src/pages/foo.md → localhost:3000/foo src/pages/foo/bar.js → localhost:3000/foo/bar

Create a file at src/pages/my-react-page.js: src/pages/my-react-page.js import React from 'react'; import Layout from '@theme/Layout'; export default function MyReactPage() { return (

My React page

This is a React page

 ); } A new page is now available at http://localhost:3000/my-react-page.
Create a file at src/pages/my-markdown-page.md: src/pages/my-markdown-page.md # My Markdown page This is a Markdown page A new page is now available at http://localhost:3000/my-markdown-page.
Docusaurus can manage multiple versions of your docs.

Release a version 1.0 of your project: npm run docusaurus docs:version 1.0 The docs folder is copied into versioned_docs/version-1.0 and versions.json is created. Your docs now have 2 versions: 1.0 at http://localhost:3000/docs/ for the version 1.0 docs current at http://localhost:3000/docs/next/ for the upcoming, unreleased docs

To navigate seamlessly across versions, add a version dropdown. Modify the docusaurus.config.js file: docusaurus.config.js export default { themeConfig: { navbar: { items: [ { type: 'docsVersionDropdown', }, ], }, }, }; The docs version dropdown appears in your navbar:

It is possible to edit versioned docs in their respective folder: versioned_docs/version-1.0/hello.md updates http://localhost:3000/docs/hello docs/hello.md updates http://localhost:3000/docs/next/hello
Docusaurus supports Markdown and a few additional features.
Markdown documents have metadata at the top called Front Matter: my-doc.md --- id: my-doc-id title: My document title description: My document description slug: /my-custom-url --- Markdown content
Markdown headings are supported using the standard “#” syntax and are automatically added to the table of contents. The number of # corresponds to the heading level. ## Headings My text
Add {/* #my-custom-id */} after the heading text to assign it an explicit anchor id, used for linking. ### Heading Ids {/_ #my-custom-id _/}

Regular Markdown links are supported, using url paths or relative file paths. Let's see how to [Create a page](/create-a-page). Let's see how to [Create a page](./create-a-page.mdx). Result: Let's see how to Create a page.

Regular Markdown images are supported. You can use absolute paths to reference images in the static directory (static/img/docusaurus.png): ![Docusaurus logo](/img/docusaurus.png) You can reference images relative to the current file as well. This is particularly useful to colocate images close to the Markdown files using them: ![Docusaurus logo](./img/docusaurus.png)

Markdown code blocks are supported with Syntax highlighting. ```jsx title="src/components/HelloDocusaurus.js" function HelloDocusaurus() { return

Hello, Docusaurus!

; } ``` src/components/HelloDocusaurus.js function HelloDocusaurus() { return

Hello, Docusaurus!

; }

Docusaurus has a special syntax to create admonitions and callouts: :::tip[My tip] Use this awesome feature option ::: :::danger[Take care] This action is dangerous ::: My tip Use this awesome feature option Take care This action is dangerous

MDX can make your documentation more interactive and allows using any React components inside Markdown: export const Highlight = ({children, color}) => ( { alert(`You clicked the color ${color} with label ${children}`) }}> {children} ); This is Docusaurus green ! This is Facebook blue ! This is Docusaurus green ! This is Facebook blue !
Docusaurus is a static-site-generator (also called Jamstack). It builds your site as simple static HTML, JavaScript and CSS files.
Build your site for production: npm run build The static files are generated in the build folder.

Test your production build locally: npm run serve The build folder is now served at http://localhost:3000/. You can now deploy the build folder almost anywhere easily, for free or very small cost (read the Deployment Guide).
Modify docusaurus.config.js to add support for the fr locale: docusaurus.config.js export default { i18n: { defaultLocale: 'en', locales: ['en', 'fr'], }, };

Copy the docs/intro.md file to the i18n/fr folder: mkdir -p i18n/fr/docusaurus-plugin-content-docs/current/ cp docs/intro.md i18n/fr/docusaurus-plugin-content-docs/current/intro.md Translate i18n/fr/docusaurus-plugin-content-docs/current/intro.md in French.

Start your site on the French locale: npm run start -- --locale fr Your localized site is accessible at http://localhost:3000/fr/ and the Getting Started page is translated. caution In development, you can only use one locale at a time.

To navigate seamlessly across languages, add a locale dropdown. Modify the docusaurus.config.js file: docusaurus.config.js export default { themeConfig: { navbar: { items: [ { type: 'localeDropdown', }, ], }, }, }; The locale dropdown now appears in your navbar:
Build your site for a specific locale: npm run build -- --locale fr Or build your site to include all the locales at once: npm run build
